texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It is well known that the emission of CO2 greenhouse gas is regarded as the most serious cause of global warming [1-3]. Several techniques are available for the separation of CO2 from flue gas streams at present, such as chemical and physical absorption, solid adsorption, carbon molecular sieve adsorption, cryogenic distillation, membrane separation, and other novel methods. In particular, membrane-based gas separation offers great opportunities for bulk separation due to its low energy consumption, easy operation, and low maintenance compared with chemical absorption or adsorption methods that require regeneration of sorbent. Moreover, the membrane process has the advantages of modular design and lightweight, making it particularly suitable for retrofit into existing processes [4-7]. Generally, the use of polymer materials for hollow-fiber membrane preparation provides attractive properties such as high packing density, self-support, and flexibility in operation.
texts.text: It is well known that the emission of CO2 greenhouse gas is regarded as the most serious cause of global warming [1-3]. Several techniques are available for the separation of CO2 from flue gas streams at present, such as chemical and physical absorption, solid adsorption, carbon molecular sieve adsorption, cryogenic distillation, membrane separation, and other novel methods. In particular, membrane-based gas separation offers great opportunities for bulk separation due to its low energy consumption, easy operation, and low maintenance compared with chemical absorption or adsorption methods that require regeneration of sorbent. Moreover, the membrane process has the advantages of modular design and lightweight, making it particularly suitable for retrofit into existing processes [4-7]. Generally, the use of polymer materials for hollow-fiber membrane preparation provides attractive properties such as high packing density, self-support, and flexibility in operation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A B S T R A C T
texts.text: A B S T R A C T

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A commercialized polysulfone (PSf) hollow-fiber membrane module was tested for CO2/N2 separation performance for application in post-combustion capture. Cost efficiency, easy module manufacturing, and efficiency in gas separation are the main advantages of using PSf hollow-fiber modules for CO2 separation. The effects of operating conditions such as temperature, pressure, and feed composition on separation performance were examined at various stage cuts. A 2-stage system including concentration of feed composition at stage 1 and production of high-purity CO2 at stage 2 was constructed to improve separation efficiency. Higher operating temperature and pressure increased CO2 permeance, but the loss of selectivity and higher energy consumption are a concern. Modules with various membrane areas were also used to test the effect of area on CO2 separation.
texts.text: A commercialized polysulfone (PSf) hollow-fiber membrane module was tested for CO2/N2 separation performance for application in post-combustion capture. Cost efficiency, easy module manufacturing, and efficiency in gas separation are the main advantages of using PSf hollow-fiber modules for CO2 separation. The effects of operating conditions such as temperature, pressure, and feed composition on separation performance were examined at various stage cuts. A 2-stage system including concentration of feed composition at stage 1 and production of high-purity CO2 at stage 2 was constructed to improve separation efficiency. Higher operating temperature and pressure increased CO2 permeance, but the loss of selectivity and higher energy consumption are a concern. Modules with various membrane areas were also used to test the effect of area on CO2 separation.

Application in industrialand utility-scale processes, including boilers, cement manufacturing, steel and aluminum production, and chemical refining can be possibly achieved by the development of a cost-effective membrane process for CO2 separation [13,14].

texts.orig: PSf has been widely used as a membrane material due to its satisfactory performance, mechanical strength, thermo-chemical stability, and low economic cost. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Optimization of module configuration in membrane gas separation was studied by Lababidi. Three systems including single-stage, two-stage, and continuous membrane column (CMC) permeators were compared in terms of optimum cost and specific operating and design characteristics [15]. Yeom et al. also investigated coupling effects to understand the difference between permeation and separation behavior in the permeation of pure and mixed gases [16]. Many other studies on CO2/N2 separation have been widely carried out in respect to high permselective polymeric materials [17,18] as well as systems analysis and design to understand and arrange the membrane process effectively [19,20].
texts.text: Optimization of module configuration in membrane gas separation was studied by Lababidi. Three systems including single-stage, two-stage, and continuous membrane column (CMC) permeators were compared in terms of optimum cost and specific operating and design characteristics [15]. Yeom et al. also investigated coupling effects to understand the difference between permeation and separation behavior in the permeation of pure and mixed gases [16]. Many other studies on CO2/N2 separation have been widely carried out in respect to high permselective polymeric materials [17,18] as well as systems analysis and design to understand and arrange the membrane process effectively [19,20].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The object of this work is to examine the performance of PSf membrane modules that have already been commercially used for oxygen/nitrogen separation. Developing our understanding of the CO2 separation performance of commercial gas separation membranes can be useful in searching for new materials, optimizing module systems, and establishing operating conditions. texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2. Experimental
texts.text: 2. Experimental

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1. Materials
texts.text: 2.1. Materials

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The PSf hollow-fiber membrane modules used in this study were kindly supplied by Airrane Co. (Daejeon, Korea). The membranes were prepared from a dope solution composed of PSf (Udel P-1800, Mw 24,000 g/mol, Solvay Advanced Polymers), N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP), teterahydrofuran (THF), and alcohol with the appropriate composition. The polymer was dried at 80 8 C in a vacuum oven before use, and other solvents were used as received. Deionized water and tap water were used as a bore solution and coagulating medium, respectively. The coating solution was prepared by mixing polydimethylsiloxane (Sylgard 184, Dow Corning) and a curing agent in n-hexane. Membrane modules of two different sizes were used for the gas separation test, and details of the modules are given in Table 1. Pure test gases with high purity of 99.8-99.99% and mixture gases of 50% CO2 and 50% N2 were supplied by Praxair.
texts.text: The PSf hollow-fiber membrane modules used in this study were kindly supplied by Airrane Co. (Daejeon, Korea). The membranes were prepared from a dope solution composed of PSf (Udel P-1800, Mw 24,000 g/mol, Solvay Advanced Polymers), N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP), teterahydrofuran (THF), and alcohol with the appropriate composition. The polymer was dried at 80 8 C in a vacuum oven before use, and other solvents were used as received. Deionized water and tap water were used as a bore solution and coagulating medium, respectively. The coating solution was prepared by mixing polydimethylsiloxane (Sylgard 184, Dow Corning) and a curing agent in n-hexane. Membrane modules of two different sizes were used for the gas separation test, and details of the modules are given in Table 1. Pure test gases with high purity of 99.8-99.99% and mixture gases of 50% CO2 and 50% N2 were supplied by Praxair.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2. Membrane and membrane module preparation
texts.text: 2.2. Membrane and membrane module preparation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Microporous PSf hollow-fiber support membranes were spun from a homogenously mixed dope solution composed of PSf, NMP, THF, and alcohol. After degassing overnight, the dope solution was extruded through a spinneret with an outer diameter of 0.4 mm and an inner diameter of 0.2 mm. Phase separation occurred both inside and outside of a membrane by coagulation water. Membranes were passed through an external coagulation bath and forwarded to a take-up bobbin. The fibers were washed in running water for 3 days to complete solventnonsolvent exchange and dried at 60 8 C for 2 days in a convection oven. The dried fibers were automatically twirled by an unwinding machine and immersed in a coating solution containing 2 wt% of Sylgard 184 in n-hexane. After dip-coating, they were continuously passed through a hot-air drying machine for 20 s and rolled up in a bobbin. A bundle of hollow fiber membranes were cut to a constant length and encased in the housing, with both ends of the fiber bundle potted with epoxy resin. When the epoxy resin became solid, the resin with the embedded fiber ends was cut straight to make a gas transfer path (i.e. feed and permeate side). The final module shape is shown in Table 1. The module size and the membrane effective area depending on the potted fiber number strongly related to their gas separation performance.
texts.text: Microporous PSf hollow-fiber support membranes were spun from a homogenously mixed dope solution composed of PSf, NMP, THF, and alcohol. After degassing overnight, the dope solution was extruded through a spinneret with an outer diameter of 0.4 mm and an inner diameter of 0.2 mm. Phase separation occurred both inside and outside of a membrane by coagulation water. Membranes were passed through an external coagulation bath and forwarded to a take-up bobbin. The fibers were washed in running water for 3 days to complete solventnonsolvent exchange and dried at 60 8 C for 2 days in a convection oven. The dried fibers were automatically twirled by an unwinding machine and immersed in a coating solution containing 2 wt% of Sylgard 184 in n-hexane. After dip-coating, they were continuously passed through a hot-air drying machine for 20 s and rolled up in a bobbin. A bundle of hollow fiber membranes were cut to a constant length and encased in the housing, with both ends of the fiber bundle potted with epoxy resin. When the epoxy resin became solid, the resin with the embedded fiber ends was cut straight to make a gas transfer path (i.e. feed and permeate side). The final module shape is shown in Table 1. The module size and the membrane effective area depending on the potted fiber number strongly related to their gas separation performance.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.3. Characterization
texts.text: 2.3. Characterization

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The membrane cross-section was observed by a field emission scanning electron microscope system (FE-SEM, Philips XL30 S FEG, Netherland). Several fibers were wetted in water and then directly immersed in liquid nitrogen. Frozen water on the membranes makes cutting easy, and clear cross-sectional fractures were obtained. The membranes were mounted on a metal holder and gold-sputtered for 30 s.
texts.text: The membrane cross-section was observed by a field emission scanning electron microscope system (FE-SEM, Philips XL30 S FEG, Netherland). Several fibers were wetted in water and then directly immersed in liquid nitrogen. Frozen water on the membranes makes cutting easy, and clear cross-sectional fractures were obtained. The membranes were mounted on a metal holder and gold-sputtered for 30 s.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The traditional bubble test method was adapted to measure gas permeance for pure and mixture gases. Fig. 1 shows the experimental set-up of the bore-side feed configuration and the counter-current flow used in this study. The feed gas at controlled pressure was passed through the bore side and the permeate flowed concurrently through the cell-side of the fibers. The permeate and residual gas flux was measured by a precise flowmeter. The permeate stream was also connected to gas chromatography (GC DS6200, Donam instruments, Korea) to confirm its composition. Pure gas permeance is the pressure normalized flux of gas A, and it is expressed in GPU (gas permeance unit) (10 � 6 cm 3 (STP)/cm 2  cmHg  s). The ideal selectivity of a gas pair A/B is defined as the permeance of gas A divided by the permeance of gas B. For a mixture gas test, the stage cut directly effects the permeate gas flux and selectivity. The stage cut is defined as the fractional feed flow permeated through the membrane, and it can be calculated from the flow ratio of permeate against feed. The fractional quantities of CO2 permeated through the membrane which is designate as CO2 recovery ( c ) is given by

texts.orig: 3. Results and discussion

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.1. Membrane morphology
texts.text: 3.1. Membrane morphology

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The intrinsic permeability of dense polymer membranes varies with the morphology of a polymer and mostly depends on the chemical structure of the polymer. Dense membranes have a thick selective layer through the entire membrane cross-section, resulting in low gas permeability. Table 2 shows the intrinsic gas permeability of CO2 and N2 gases on PSf and PDMS dense membranes [22,23]. Asymmetric membranes composed of a thin skin layer to allow high gas fluxes and a porous support layer to provide appropriate mechanical strength are favorably used for industrial gas separation. The simplest preparation method for an ultra-thin skin layer membrane to increase gas fluxes is a composite membrane formed by dip coating a suitable substrate with a thin layer of the selective polymer. The morphology of the hollow-fiber composite membrane used in this study is shown in
texts.text: The intrinsic permeability of dense polymer membranes varies with the morphology of a polymer and mostly depends on the chemical structure of the polymer. Dense membranes have a thick selective layer through the entire membrane cross-section, resulting in low gas permeability. Table 2 shows the intrinsic gas permeability of CO2 and N2 gases on PSf and PDMS dense membranes [22,23]. Asymmetric membranes composed of a thin skin layer to allow high gas fluxes and a porous support layer to provide appropriate mechanical strength are favorably used for industrial gas separation. The simplest preparation method for an ultra-thin skin layer membrane to increase gas fluxes is a composite membrane formed by dip coating a suitable substrate with a thin layer of the selective polymer. The morphology of the hollow-fiber composite membrane used in this study is shown in

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 1. The integrally skinned asymmetric membrane and rubbery selective coating layer were made of PSf and PDMS, respectively. A sponge like structure on the bottom-side, macrovoids formed toward the top-side and a dense skin layer on the top by the evaporation of a volatile additive such as alcohol were observed in membrane cross-section images. Several defects on the membrane surface were covered by PDMS dip coating. The inner and outer diameter of the membrane is around 300 m m and 450 m m, respectively. The average skin layer thickness is approximately 0.5 m m (Table 3). Fig. 2 shows the schematic diagram of gas permeation test for pure and mixure gases used in this study.
texts.text: Fig. 1. The integrally skinned asymmetric membrane and rubbery selective coating layer were made of PSf and PDMS, respectively. A sponge like structure on the bottom-side, macrovoids formed toward the top-side and a dense skin layer on the top by the evaporation of a volatile additive such as alcohol were observed in membrane cross-section images. Several defects on the membrane surface were covered by PDMS dip coating. The inner and outer diameter of the membrane is around 300 m m and 450 m m, respectively. The average skin layer thickness is approximately 0.5 m m (Table 3). Fig. 2 shows the schematic diagram of gas permeation test for pure and mixure gases used in this study.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.2. Effect of operating temperature
texts.text: 3.2. Effect of operating temperature

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 3 shows the permeance and selectivity of pure gases on the hollow-fiber membrane module of Meritair 1507P at various operating temperatures. Increasing gas permeance and decreasing gas selectivity as the temperature increased were observed following the Arrhenius equation. The decreasing selectivity of various gases over N2 with increasing temperature can be explained in terms of a difference in permeation activation energy between gases. The higher activation energy of N2 increases its permeability significantly, so that the selectivity to other gases over N2 declines as the temperature increases [24]. The constant permeation tendency revealed that the composite membrane is defect-free and a solution-diffusion mechanism for gas permeation is applied. However, the selectivity of the composite membrane at 40 8 C is around 22, which is much higher than that of PDMS intrinsic selectivity. This result implies that the gas separation occurred on the dense skin layer of PSf formed by solvent evaporation, providing similar intrinsic selectivity as PSf. In other words, the PDMS coating layer only play a role as a cover for unexpected defects on the membrane surface to maintain its selectivity. The defect-free thin skin layer with a mechanically stable porous substrate can offer considerably higher gas permeance than the dense membrane. The composite membrane exhibited a CO2 permeance of 120 GPU and CO2/N2 selectivity of 26.4. Gas permeance through the skin layer increased with an increase in the operating temperature due to increase in the activation energy of permeate gases, whereas the selectivity of gas pairs decreased. As the result, high gas productivity can be obtained at higher operating temperatures, but the gas purity will be reduced.
texts.text: Fig. 3 shows the permeance and selectivity of pure gases on the hollow-fiber membrane module of Meritair 1507P at various operating temperatures. Increasing gas permeance and decreasing gas selectivity as the temperature increased were observed following the Arrhenius equation. The decreasing selectivity of various gases over N2 with increasing temperature can be explained in terms of a difference in permeation activation energy between gases. The higher activation energy of N2 increases its permeability significantly, so that the selectivity to other gases over N2 declines as the temperature increases [24]. The constant permeation tendency revealed that the composite membrane is defect-free and a solution-diffusion mechanism for gas permeation is applied. However, the selectivity of the composite membrane at 40 8 C is around 22, which is much higher than that of PDMS intrinsic selectivity. This result implies that the gas separation occurred on the dense skin layer of PSf formed by solvent evaporation, providing similar intrinsic selectivity as PSf. In other words, the PDMS coating layer only play a role as a cover for unexpected defects on the membrane surface to maintain its selectivity. The defect-free thin skin layer with a mechanically stable porous substrate can offer considerably higher gas permeance than the dense membrane. The composite membrane exhibited a CO2 permeance of 120 GPU and CO2/N2 selectivity of 26.4. Gas permeance through the skin layer increased with an increase in the operating temperature due to increase in the activation energy of permeate gases, whereas the selectivity of gas pairs decreased. As the result, high gas productivity can be obtained at higher operating temperatures, but the gas purity will be reduced.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For separation of a mixture gas composed of CO2/N2 = 50/50%, the changes of CO2 concentration and the recovery ratio were examined at different stage-cuts using a Meritair 1008P module. The stage cut is determined as the ratio of the permeate flux to the feed flux. As shown in Fig. 4, the increasing stage cut decreased the CO2 concentration and recovery ratio in permeate. The CO2 purity at 25 8 C is 5% higher than the CO2 purity at 50 8 C in the range of a stage-cut less than 0.5. The stage cuts to obtain 78% of CO2 concentration at 25 8 C and 50 8 C are determined to be 0.5 and 0.1, respectively, and this means that the CO2 gas permeance to obtain the same CO2 purity is doubled at 25 8 C compared to 50 8 C. As summarized in Fig. 5, the effects of CO2 permeate flux, purity, and recovery on membrane performance should be carefully considered to determine the optimum operating temperature for industrial application.
texts.text: For separation of a mixture gas composed of CO2/N2 = 50/50%, the changes of CO2 concentration and the recovery ratio were examined at different stage-cuts using a Meritair 1008P module. The stage cut is determined as the ratio of the permeate flux to the feed flux. As shown in Fig. 4, the increasing stage cut decreased the CO2 concentration and recovery ratio in permeate. The CO2 purity at 25 8 C is 5% higher than the CO2 purity at 50 8 C in the range of a stage-cut less than 0.5. The stage cuts to obtain 78% of CO2 concentration at 25 8 C and 50 8 C are determined to be 0.5 and 0.1, respectively, and this means that the CO2 gas permeance to obtain the same CO2 purity is doubled at 25 8 C compared to 50 8 C. As summarized in Fig. 5, the effects of CO2 permeate flux, purity, and recovery on membrane performance should be carefully considered to determine the optimum operating temperature for industrial application.

texts.orig: Fig. 4. The effects of operating temperature on the CO2 concentration and recovery ratio. Feed pressure, 8 kgf/cm 2 (solid -CO2 concentration, open -recovery ratio).
texts.text: Fig. 4. The effects of operating temperature on the CO2 concentration and recovery ratio. Feed pressure, 8 kgf/cm 2 (solid -CO2 concentration, open -recovery ratio).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: compression at the feed side. The result presented in Fig. 6 shows no changes in CO2 concentration and recovery ratio as a function of stage cut. Since separation occurs at the skin layer of glassy PSf, it is expected to be less sensitive to pressures changes than the rubbery PDMS [25]. Although the increase of stage cut decreases CO2 concentration at both pressures, the depletion curve at 9 bar is slightly sharper, indicating the pressure effect on the CO2 concentration. A more significant effect of pressure difference can be expressed by the CO2 flux and separation factor as shown in Fig. 7. Higher operating pressures at the feed side showed increased CO2 flux with a constant separation factor. An increased operating pressure may be preferable to obtain high gas flux which directly relates to membrane productivity. However, the capital cost and the energy consumption associated with compression should be considered in selecting the optimum operating condition, and some researchers have been working on cost estimation for membrane processes [26,27].
texts.text: compression at the feed side. The result presented in Fig. 6 shows no changes in CO2 concentration and recovery ratio as a function of stage cut. Since separation occurs at the skin layer of glassy PSf, it is expected to be less sensitive to pressures changes than the rubbery PDMS [25]. Although the increase of stage cut decreases CO2 concentration at both pressures, the depletion curve at 9 bar is slightly sharper, indicating the pressure effect on the CO2 concentration. A more significant effect of pressure difference can be expressed by the CO2 flux and separation factor as shown in Fig. 7. Higher operating pressures at the feed side showed increased CO2 flux with a constant separation factor. An increased operating pressure may be preferable to obtain high gas flux which directly relates to membrane productivity. However, the capital cost and the energy consumption associated with compression should be considered in selecting the optimum operating condition, and some researchers have been working on cost estimation for membrane processes [26,27].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.4. Effect of feed composition
texts.text: 3.4. Effect of feed composition

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figs. 8 and 9 show the changes of CO2 permeate, separation factor, CO2 concentration, and recovery ratio with increasing stage cut. When the CO2 concentration increase from 30% to 50% in the feed stream, the CO2 flux improved from 20.6 GPU to 35.0 GPU with a rise of CO2 concentration from 70.5% to 81.6% in the permeate at a stage cut of 0.1. The reduced CO2 permeance with a decrease in CO2 concentration in the feed can be explained by a coupling effect which is often due to the presence of other gas, in spite of the fact that the membrane properties such as permeability and selectivity should be constant independent of feed composition. Generally, the coupling effect on permeation is strongly related to deviations in the solubility coefficient, while the diffusivity coefficient is not affected by the presence of other gas [16,28]. In the present work, the polar CO2 gas molecule is favorably absorbed in the limited absorption sites in the polymer,

When the CO2 concentration increase from 30% to 50% in the feed stream, the CO2 flux improved from 20.6 GPU to 35.0 GPU with a rise of CO2 concentration from 70.5% to 81.6% in the permeate at a stage cut of 0.1. The reduced CO2 permeance with a decrease in CO2 concentration in the feed can be explained by a coupling effect which is often due to the presence of other gas, in spite of the fact that the membrane properties such as permeability and selectivity should be constant independent of feed composition. Generally, the coupling effect on permeation is strongly related to deviations in the solubility coefficient, while the diffusivity coefficient is not affected by the presence of other gas [16,28]. In the present work, the polar CO2 gas molecule is favorably absorbed in the limited absorption sites in the polymer,

texts.label: text
texts.orig: due to its higher solubility and faster permeation rate than N2. As a result, the solubility coefficient increases while the diffusivity coefficient is constant with increasing CO2 concentration in the feed, resulting in an increase in CO2 permeance and concentration in permeate. The selectivity difference between pure gas and mixture gas also can be understood in the same context.
texts.text: due to its higher solubility and faster permeation rate than N2. As a result, the solubility coefficient increases while the diffusivity coefficient is constant with increasing CO2 concentration in the feed, resulting in an increase in CO2 permeance and concentration in permeate. The selectivity difference between pure gas and mixture gas also can be understood in the same context.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The results from the feed composition effect on membrane performance indicate that CO2 enrichment in feed gas is necessary to improve separation efficiency. Therefore, a multi-stage membrane system is required to produce higher CO2 concentrations in the feed as well as lower energy consumption [29].
texts.text: The results from the feed composition effect on membrane performance indicate that CO2 enrichment in feed gas is necessary to improve separation efficiency. Therefore, a multi-stage membrane system is required to produce higher CO2 concentrations in the feed as well as lower energy consumption [29].

texts.orig: 3.5. Membrane stage
texts.text: 3.5. Membrane stage

texts.label: text

texts.label: text
texts.orig: to air injection during combustion. The low feed pressure and CO2 concentration are major shortcomings for a membrane process [10,27]. Therefore, multi-stage gas separation is considerable to obtain the advantages of a membrane with high permeability and selectivity.
texts.text: to air injection during combustion. The low feed pressure and CO2 concentration are major shortcomings for a membrane process [10,27]. Therefore, multi-stage gas separation is considerable to obtain the advantages of a membrane with high permeability and selectivity.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A 2-stage membrane system assembled using various module sizes was examined to obtain high CO2 concentration over 90% and the results were organized in Table 3. Meritair 1507P which has an effective membrane area of 6400 cm 2 was installed at stage 1, and each Meritair 1507P and Meritair 1008P (effective membrane area of 830 cm 2 ) was connected to the first module. At the first stage, the module operated under 8 kgf/cm 2 and produced concentrated CO2 gas to 80% with gas permeation of 21-28 GPU through stage cuts of 0.4-0.6. The required pressure to obtain CO2 purity over 90% varied at stage 2 depending on the membrane area. Increased CO2 purity after stage 1 treatment helped to enhance module efficiency for both second modules. When Meritair 1008P was applied, the desired CO2 purity was obtained at relatively high operating pressure with a low stage cut compared to Meritair 1507P. In an economic sense, optimization of the overall cost should be balanced between the capital cost (membrane area, additional compressor, etc.) and the operating cost (required pressure). In this study, the use of the cost effective material of PSf and its ease of manufacture for various membrane modules are its main advantages for multi-stage membrane systems [23,29]. Using a larger membrane area was more effective than increasing operating pressure for CO2 enrichment with purities over 90%.
texts.text: A 2-stage membrane system assembled using various module sizes was examined to obtain high CO2 concentration over 90% and the results were organized in Table 3. Meritair 1507P which has an effective membrane area of 6400 cm 2 was installed at stage 1, and each Meritair 1507P and Meritair 1008P (effective membrane area of 830 cm 2 ) was connected to the first module. At the first stage, the module operated under 8 kgf/cm 2 and produced concentrated CO2 gas to 80% with gas permeation of 21-28 GPU through stage cuts of 0.4-0.6. The required pressure to obtain CO2 purity over 90% varied at stage 2 depending on the membrane area. Increased CO2 purity after stage 1 treatment helped to enhance module efficiency for both second modules. When Meritair 1008P was applied, the desired CO2 purity was obtained at relatively high operating pressure with a low stage cut compared to Meritair 1507P. In an economic sense, optimization of the overall cost should be balanced between the capital cost (membrane area, additional compressor, etc.) and the operating cost (required pressure). In this study, the use of the cost effective material of PSf and its ease of manufacture for various membrane modules are its main advantages for multi-stage membrane systems [23,29]. Using a larger membrane area was more effective than increasing operating pressure for CO2 enrichment with purities over 90%.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusions
texts.text: 4. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Commercially available PSf hollow-fiber modules were tested to evaluate their CO2 separation performance under various operating conditions. CO2 permeance and CO2/N2 selectivity for pure gas test were around 120 GPU and 26.4, respectively, and membrane selectivity was strongly related to the instinct selectivity of PSf, indicating that separation may occur at the thin skin layer of PSf. Increases in operating temperature for the mixture gas test led to increases in gas permeance with loss of selectivity due to increasing activation energy of permeate gases. The CO2permeance was almost doubled at 25 8 C compared to 50 8 C to produce the same CO2 purity of 78%. Increasing operating pressure resulted in negligible changes in CO2 purity and recovery ratio as a function of stage cut because separation take place at the skin layer of less sensitive PSf. However, higher operating pressure at the feed side resulted in increased CO2 flux with constant separation factor. The reduced CO2 permeance with decreased CO2 concentration in the feed resulted in increased CO2 permeance and concentration in permeate. This may be explained by a coupling effect caused by the different selectivity coefficient. A 2-stage membrane separation system was adapted to enrich CO2 in feed gas for high separation efficiency. Several economic advantages of using PSf and its ease of manufacture for various membrane modules make it an excellent choice for multistage membrane systems.
texts.text: Commercially available PSf hollow-fiber modules were tested to evaluate their CO2 separation performance under various operating conditions. CO2 permeance and CO2/N2 selectivity for pure gas test were around 120 GPU and 26.4, respectively, and membrane selectivity was strongly related to the instinct selectivity of PSf, indicating that separation may occur at the thin skin layer of PSf. Increases in operating temperature for the mixture gas test led to increases in gas permeance with loss of selectivity due to increasing activation energy of permeate gases. The CO2permeance was almost doubled at 25 8 C compared to 50 8 C to produce the same CO2 purity of 78%. Increasing operating pressure resulted in negligible changes in CO2 purity and recovery ratio as a function of stage cut because separation take place at the skin layer of less sensitive PSf. However, higher operating pressure at the feed side resulted in increased CO2 flux with constant separation factor. The reduced CO2 permeance with decreased CO2 concentration in the feed resulted in increased CO2 permeance and concentration in permeate. This may be explained by a coupling effect caused by the different selectivity coefficient. A 2-stage membrane separation system was adapted to enrich CO2 in feed gas for high separation efficiency. Several economic advantages of using PSf and its ease of manufacture for various membrane modules make it an excellent choice for multistage membrane systems.
